news_summary: Today's space news highlights remarkable discoveries of Earth-like planetary conditions, international collaboration on space exploration, advances in understanding Earth's impact on space weather, and stunning observations of towering red auroras.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité spatiale d'aujourd'hui met en avant des découvertes remarquables concernant des conditions planétaires similaires à celles de la Terre, la collaboration internationale en matière d'exploration spatiale, les progrès réalisés dans la compréhension de l'impact de la Terre sur la météorologie spatiale, ainsi que de superbes observations d'aurores rouges gigantesques.
news_summary_es: Las noticias espaciales de hoy destacan descubrimientos notables sobre condiciones planetarias similares a las de la Tierra, la colaboración internacional en la exploración espacial, los avances en la comprensión del impacto de la Tierra en el clima espacial y unas impresionantes observaciones de imponentes auroras rojas.

articles.title: James Webb discovers a rare giant planet with surprisingly Earth-like temperatures
articles.summary: Astronomers using NASA’s James Webb Space Telescope have uncovered a rare world unlike anything in our solar system — a giant planet about the size of Saturn with surprisingly Earth-like temperatures and an atmosphere packed with methane. The planet, TOI-199b, sits more than 330 light-years away and is one of the first known “temperate” gas giants ever studied in detail.
articles.content:
Astronomers using NASA's James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) have taken a close look at the atmosphere of a rare giant planet with temperatures surprisingly similar to those on Earth. The Saturn sized world, known as TOI-199b, contains methane in its atmosphere, according to a new study.

The discovery stands out because giant planets are usually found at temperature extremes. In our solar system, Jupiter and Saturn are extremely cold because they orbit far from the sun. Many giant exoplanets discovered beyond our solar system are "hot Jupiters," worlds that circle very close to their stars and reach blistering temperatures of thousands of degrees.

TOI-199b falls somewhere in between. It is one of only a small number of known temperate giant planets, and researchers say this is the first time the atmosphere of one has been studied in such detail.

The findings could help scientists improve models of how planets and atmospheres form and evolve. Researchers also say the work may eventually provide new insight into Earth's own atmosphere.

The study, led by scientists from Penn State and NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL) at the California Institute of Technology, was published May 20 in the Astronomical Journal.

"One of the main advantages of studies of planets beyond our solar system, known as exoplanets, is the ability to study many different types of planets -- especially ones that we don't see in the solar system -- to learn about how planetary systems form and evolve," said Renyu Hu, associate professor of astronomy and astrophysics in the Penn State Eberly College of Science and leader of the research team. "Since the first exoplanet was discovered in 1992 by a team that included Aleksander Wolszczan at Penn State, astronomers have found thousands of exoplanets. But only a few giant, temperate exoplanets are known and this is the first time that we have been able to study the atmosphere of one of them in detail."

A Giant Planet With Surprisingly Mild Temperatures

TOI-199b orbits a star located more than 330 light years from Earth and completes one orbit roughly every 100 days.

The planet's temperature is estimated to be around 175 degrees Fahrenheit. While still extremely hot by everyday standards, it is much cooler than most giant exoplanets studied so far. For comparison, temperatures inside parked cars sitting in direct sunlight can easily climb to similar levels on Earth.

That makes TOI-199b far milder than hot Jupiters, while also being dramatically warmer than the frozen gas giants in our own solar system, where temperatures can plunge hundreds of degrees below zero.

How JWST Studied the Planet's Atmosphere

To investigate the atmosphere of TOI-199b, researchers used a method called transmission spectroscopy. This technique allows astronomers to study starlight that passes through a planet's atmosphere while the planet moves across the face of its star from the telescope's point of view.

JWST separates the star's light into different wavelengths, similar to how a prism splits white light into a rainbow.

"As a planet passes in front of its star, some of the star's light passes through the planet's atmosphere where it interacts with the elements and molecules in the atmosphere," said Aaron Bello-Arufe, a postdoctoral researcher at JPL and the first author of the paper. "Specific elements will absorb specific wavelengths of light, creating a fingerprint in the spectrum of light that JWST detects that reflects the atmosphere's composition."

Researchers first collected about 20 continuous hours of observations to establish a baseline measurement of the star's light. The planetary transit itself lasted about seven hours, much longer than the transits typically seen for hot Jupiters, which can last an hour or less.

Scientists then compared the light spectrum recorded during the transit with the baseline measurements. The differences revealed which wavelengths were absorbed by the planet's atmosphere, allowing the team to identify the gases present.

Methane Detected in TOI-199b's Atmosphere

"When we compared the spectra during the transit to the baseline, we saw that the atmosphere blocked the wavelengths of starlight absorbed by methane," Bello-Arufe said. "Models for the composition of temperate, gas-giant exoplanets had predicted that they would contain methane, so it is good to get confirmation that our theories are accurate."

In addition to methane, the observations also hinted at the presence of ammonia and carbon dioxide.

"With additional observations of this planet, we could establish the relative abundance of these various gases in its atmosphere," Hu said. "This more complete picture of a temperate gas giant's atmosphere can then be used to improve our models and potentially better understand how planets and their atmospheres form and evolve, including for Earth. The success of this first study of a temperate giant planet's atmosphere also gives us confidence to dedicate more resources and observation time to study other similar planets. We can then see if this planet is unique or if there are general shared characteristics for this type of planet."

The research team also included scientists from Arizona State University, Johns Hopkins University, the Carnegie Institution for Science, the California Institute of Technology, and the University of California Santa Cruz.

NASA funded the research through a grant from the Space Telescope Science Institute.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery of a rare temperate gas giant exoplanet with Earth-like temperatures, studied in detail for the first time using the James Webb Space Telescope. This discovery advances our understanding of planetary atmospheres and formation, with potential broad implications for planetary science and insights into Earth's atmosphere, thus having meaningful real-world impact and broad significance.
articles.personality_title: James Webb spots a rare giant planet with Earth-like temperatures
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists usually find giant planets either very hot or very cold. Our own Jupiter and Saturn are very cold, while many giant planets found outside our solar system, called exoplanets, are extremely hot. 

**What happened** – Using NASA’s James Webb Space Telescope, astronomers studied a giant planet named TOI-199b, about the size of Saturn, located over 330 light-years away. Unlike other giant planets, TOI-199b has temperatures closer to Earth’s and an atmosphere filled with methane. This is the first time scientists have examined the atmosphere of such a “temperate” giant planet in detail.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it helps scientists understand how planets and their atmospheres form and change over time. Studying TOI-199b’s atmosphere, which contains methane and possibly ammonia and carbon dioxide, could also teach us more about Earth’s own atmosphere. It shows that planets can have very different conditions than those we see in our solar system.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to study TOI-199b more closely to measure the amounts of gases in its atmosphere. They also want to find and study other temperate giant planets to see if TOI-199b is unique or part of a group with similar features.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s James Webb Space Telescope has revealed a rare giant planet with Earth-like temperatures and methane, opening new paths to understanding planets beyond our solar system.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le télescope James Webb découvre une planète géante rare aux températures proches de celles de la Terre
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques trouvent habituellement les planètes géantes très chaudes ou très froides. Jupiter et Saturne dans notre système solaire sont très froides, tandis que beaucoup de planètes géantes en dehors de notre système, appelées exoplanètes, sont extrêmement chaudes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Grâce au télescope spatial James Webb de la NASA, des astronomes ont étudié une planète géante appelée TOI-199b, de la taille de Saturne, située à plus de 330 années-lumière. Contrairement aux autres planètes géantes, TOI-199b a des températures proches de celles de la Terre et une atmosphère riche en méthane. C’est la première fois que les scientifiques examinent en détail l’atmosphère d’une planète géante « tempérée ».

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle aide les scientifiques à comprendre comment les planètes et leurs atmosphères se forment et évoluent. Étudier l’atmosphère de TOI-199b, qui contient du méthane et peut-être de l’ammoniac et du dioxyde de carbone, pourrait aussi en apprendre davantage sur l’atmosphère terrestre. Elle montre que les planètes peuvent avoir des conditions très différentes de celles de notre système solaire.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs comptent étudier TOI-199b plus en détail pour mesurer la quantité des gaz dans son atmosphère. Ils veulent aussi trouver et étudier d’autres planètes géantes tempérées pour voir si TOI-199b est unique ou fait partie d’un groupe avec des caractéristiques similaires.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Le télescope James Webb de la NASA a révélé une planète géante rare aux températures proches de celles de la Terre et riche en méthane, ouvrant de nouvelles voies pour comprendre les planètes au-delà de notre système solaire.
articles.personality_title_es: James Webb descubre un raro planeta gigante con temperaturas similares a las de la Tierra
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos suelen encontrar planetas gigantes que son muy calientes o muy fríos. En nuestro sistema solar, Júpiter y Saturno son muy fríos, mientras que muchos planetas gigantes fuera de nuestro sistema, llamados exoplanetas, son extremadamente calientes.

**Qué pasó** – Usando el telescopio espacial James Webb de la NASA, los astrónomos estudiaron un planeta gigante llamado TOI-199b, del tamaño de Saturno, ubicado a más de 330 años luz. A diferencia de otros planetas gigantes, TOI-199b tiene temperaturas parecidas a las de la Tierra y una atmósfera llena de metano. Es la primera vez que los científicos examinan en detalle la atmósfera de un planeta gigante “templado”.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque ayuda a los científicos a entender cómo se forman y cambian los planetas y sus atmósferas. Estudiar la atmósfera de TOI-199b, que contiene metano y posiblemente amoníaco y dióxido de carbono, también podría enseñarnos más sobre la atmósfera de la Tierra. Muestra que los planetas pueden tener condiciones muy diferentes a las que vemos en nuestro sistema solar.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean estudiar más a fondo a TOI-199b para medir la cantidad de gases en su atmósfera. También quieren encontrar y estudiar otros planetas gigantes templados para ver si TOI-199b es único o parte de un grupo con características similares.

**Conclusión en una frase** – El telescopio James Webb de la NASA ha descubierto un raro planeta gigante con temperaturas similares a las de la Tierra y metano, abriendo nuevos caminos para entender planetas más allá de nuestro sistema solar.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a large, glowing Saturn-sized planet with soft swirling clouds tinted with hints of methane blue and amber, gently orbiting a distant, radiant star, with delicate beams of starlight filtering through the planet’s translucent atmosphere, revealing subtle patterns symbolizing the presence of methane and other gases.

articles.title: NASA Joins Artemis Accords Workshop as Global Signings Rise
articles.summary: The United States participated in an Artemis Accords workshop in Lima, Peru, last week, following a new wave of nations committing to safe and responsible exploration of the Moon, Mars, and beyond. Leading up to the event, six countries, including Latvia, Jordan, Morocco, Malta, Ireland, and Paraguay, joined the growing coalition of Artemis Accords signatories […]
articles.content:
Representatives of the Artemis Accords signatories including the United States, led by NASA and the U.S. Department of State, met May 13–14, 2026, in Lima for the fourth annual Artemis Accords workshop. Credit: Ministry of Foreign Affairs of Peru and Peruvian Space Agency (CONIDA)

The United States participated in an Artemis Accords workshop in Lima, Peru, last week, following a new wave of nations committing to safe and responsible exploration of the Moon, Mars, and beyond.

Leading up to the event, six countries, including Latvia, Jordan, Morocco, Malta, Ireland, and Paraguay, joined the growing coalition of Artemis Accords signatories during ceremonies held at NASA Headquarters and abroad. This brings the total number of Artemis Accords signatories to 67 like-minded nations.

“This gathering showcases the remarkable global momentum behind the Artemis Accords and our Artemis program,” said NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman. “The Artemis Accords were created in President Trump’s first term and, as we execute his National Space Policy, we are putting these principles into practice. By aligning our capabilities, acting with urgency, and moving forward as partners, these signatory countries will help shape the future, not from the sidelines, but as essential contributors to humanity’s first permanent outpost on the Moon. Each and every Artemis Accords signatory has the opportunity to play a meaningful role with NASA as we work together to build a sustained human presence on the surface of the Moon.”

On May 13-14, representatives from NASA and the U.S. Department of State joined dozens of counterparts from 30 countries, including several of the newest signatories, for technical discussions and a tabletop exercise focused on operating in complex lunar environments.

Peru hosted the fourth annual workshop, marking the first time the gathering has taken place in South America.

“One of our objectives in hosting this edition of the workshops in our country was to increase regional participation,” said Maj. Gen. Roberto Melgar Sheen, director of Peruvian Space Agency (CONIDA). “I am pleased to say that we have achieved this: All South American signatory countries are taking part in this event, with 90% participating in person and 10% virtually.”

The Artemis Accords community reviewed planned lunar landing and orbiting missions from all the signatories in attendance. With more than a dozen lunar landing missions expected over the next 18 months, last week’s discussions and tabletop exercises focused on non-interference, interoperability, release of scientific data, orbital debris and mitigation. These conversations included a presentation on NASA’s exploration plan, which accelerates the agency’s missions to the Moon. Artemis Accords signatories now have expanded opportunities to support NASA’s Moon Base and deepen their participation in the broader Artemis program, following the agency’s Ignition event on March 24.

“Peru joined the Artemis Accords in 2024, aiming to participate in a cutting-edge dialogue mechanism that addresses global trends in space exploration. We aspire to forge cooperative ties with the signatories of the Artemis Accords that contribute to the scientific and aerospace development of our country,” said Peru’s Vice Minister of Foreign Affairs Ambassador Felix Denegri about the workshop.

During the first Trump Administration, the United States, led by NASA and the U.S. State Department, joined with seven other founding nations in 2020 to establish the Artemis Accords in response to the growing interest in lunar activities by both governments and private companies. Today, countries representing every region of the world have committed to responsible principles for exploration.

Signing the Artemis Accords means a commitment to the peaceful and transparent exploration of space; rendering aid to those in need; enabling access to scientific data; ensuring activities do not interfere with those of others; and preserving historically significant sites and artifacts by developing best practices.

More countries are expected to sign the Artemis Accords in the months and years ahead, as NASA continues its work to establish a safe, peaceful, and prosperous future in space.

For more information about the Artemis Accords, visit:

https://www.nasa.gov/artemis-accords

-end-

Camille Gallo / Elizabeth Shaw

Headquarters, Washington

202-358-1600

camille.m.gallo@nasa.gov / elizabeth.a.shaw@nasa.gov
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant global collaboration through the Artemis Accords, with 67 nations committing to safe, responsible, and peaceful exploration of the Moon, Mars, and beyond. This international agreement promotes transparency, scientific cooperation, and sustainable space exploration, which has broad and meaningful impact on humanity's future in space. The article provides detailed context about the workshop, new signatories, and the principles guiding this effort, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: NASA and 67 Countries Unite for Safe Moon and Mars Exploration
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Artemis Accords is an agreement started by the United States and other countries to explore the Moon, Mars, and space responsibly and peacefully. It sets rules to make sure countries work together and respect each other's activities in space.

**What happened** – In May 2026, NASA and the U.S. Department of State joined a workshop in Lima, Peru, with representatives from 30 countries. Six new countries—Latvia, Jordan, Morocco, Malta, Ireland, and Paraguay—recently signed the Artemis Accords, bringing the total to 67 nations. Peru hosted the workshop for the first time in South America, where participants talked about upcoming Moon missions and how to work together safely.

**Impact** – This is the largest global group ever committed to peaceful space exploration. The countries agreed to share scientific data, avoid interfering with each other’s missions, and protect important sites on the Moon. With dozens of Moon landings planned soon, this teamwork helps prevent accidents and supports building a permanent human base on the Moon.

**What's next step** – More countries are expected to join the Artemis Accords in the future. NASA and its partners will continue working together to carry out new missions, share knowledge, and explore space safely. Workshops like the one in Peru will help countries prepare for complex space activities.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA and 67 countries are working together under the Artemis Accords to explore the Moon and Mars safely, sharing knowledge and protecting space for everyone.
articles.personality_title_fr: La NASA et 67 pays s’unissent pour une exploration sûre de la Lune et de Mars
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les Accords Artemis sont un accord lancé par les États-Unis et d’autres pays pour explorer la Lune, Mars et l’espace de manière responsable et pacifique. Ils établissent des règles pour que les pays collaborent et respectent les activités des uns et des autres dans l’espace.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En mai 2026, la NASA et le Département d’État américain ont participé à un atelier à Lima, au Pérou, avec des représentants de 30 pays. Six nouveaux pays – la Lettonie, la Jordanie, le Maroc, Malte, l’Irlande et le Paraguay – ont récemment signé les Accords Artemis, portant le total à 67 nations. Le Pérou a accueilli l’atelier pour la première fois en Amérique du Sud, où les participants ont discuté des missions lunaires à venir et de la manière de travailler ensemble en toute sécurité.

**Impact** – Il s’agit du plus grand groupe mondial jamais engagé pour une exploration spatiale pacifique. Les pays ont accepté de partager leurs données scientifiques, d’éviter de perturber les missions des autres et de protéger les sites importants sur la Lune. Avec des dizaines d’atterrissages lunaires prévus bientôt, cette collaboration aide à prévenir les accidents et soutient la construction d’une base humaine permanente sur la Lune.

**Prochaine étape** – D’autres pays devraient rejoindre les Accords Artemis à l’avenir. La NASA et ses partenaires continueront de travailler ensemble pour réaliser de nouvelles missions, partager leurs connaissances et explorer l’espace en toute sécurité. Des ateliers comme celui du Pérou aideront les pays à se préparer pour des activités spatiales complexes.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La NASA et 67 pays collaborent dans le cadre des Accords Artemis pour explorer la Lune et Mars en toute sécurité, en partageant leurs connaissances et en protégeant l’espace pour tous.
articles.personality_title_es: La NASA y 67 países se unen para una exploración segura de la Luna y Marte
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los Acuerdos Artemis son un pacto iniciado por Estados Unidos y otros países para explorar la Luna, Marte y el espacio de manera responsable y pacífica. Establecen reglas para que los países colaboren y respeten las actividades de los demás en el espacio.

**Qué pasó** – En mayo de 2026, la NASA y el Departamento de Estado de EE. UU. participaron en un taller en Lima, Perú, con representantes de 30 países. Seis nuevos países—Letonia, Jordania, Marruecos, Malta, Irlanda y Paraguay—firmaron recientemente los Acuerdos Artemis, sumando un total de 67 naciones. Perú fue sede del taller por primera vez en Sudamérica, donde los participantes hablaron sobre las próximas misiones lunares y cómo trabajar juntos de forma segura.

**Impacto** – Este es el grupo global más grande comprometido con la exploración pacífica del espacio. Los países acordaron compartir datos científicos, evitar interferir en las misiones de otros y proteger sitios importantes en la Luna. Con docenas de aterrizajes lunares planeados pronto, esta colaboración ayuda a prevenir accidentes y apoya la construcción de una base humana permanente en la Luna.

**Próximo paso** – Se espera que más países se unan a los Acuerdos Artemis en el futuro. La NASA y sus socios seguirán trabajando juntos para realizar nuevas misiones, compartir conocimientos y explorar el espacio de manera segura. Talleres como el de Perú ayudarán a los países a prepararse para actividades espaciales complejas.

**Resumen en una frase** – La NASA y 67 países trabajan juntos bajo los Acuerdos Artemis para explorar la Luna y Marte de forma segura, compartiendo conocimientos y protegiendo el espacio para todos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing lunar surface with interconnected, colorful flags representing 67 nations arranged around a stylized, futuristic Moon base, under a starry sky where abstract silhouettes of spacecraft orbit peacefully, symbolizing global cooperation and shared commitment to safe and responsible space exploration.

articles.title: NASA’s AWE Completes Mission to Study Earth’s Effect on Space Weather
articles.summary: On May 21, ground controllers powered down NASA’s AWE (Atmospheric Waves Experiment) instrument, bringing the data collection phase of the mission to a successful and scheduled end, surpassing its planned two-year mission. Installed on the exterior of the International Space Station since November 2023, AWE studied atmospheric gravity waves, which are giant ripples in the […]
articles.content:
On May 21, ground controllers powered down NASA’s AWE (Atmospheric Waves Experiment) instrument, bringing the data collection phase of the mission to a successful and scheduled end, surpassing its planned two-year mission.

Installed on the exterior of the International Space Station since November 2023, AWE studied atmospheric gravity waves, which are giant ripples in the atmosphere caused by strong winds flowing over tall mountains or by violent weather events, such as tornadoes, thunderstorms, and hurricanes. The AWE instrument looked for these waves in colorful bands of light in Earth’s atmosphere, called airglow. Funded by NASA’s Heliophysics Division, AWE investigated how atmospheric gravity waves propagate upward to space and contribute to space weather — conditions in space that can disrupt satellites, as well as navigation and communications signals.

“The AWE mission has proven that our atmosphere is not a ceiling, but a living, breathing ocean in the sky,” said Joe Westlake, director of NASA’s Heliophysics Division at NASA Headquarters in Washington. “For the first time, we can see how a thunderstorm in the Midwest, a hurricane over Florida, or a wind gust over the Andes sends invisible ripples — atmospheric gravity waves — crashing into the edge of space like waves hitting a shoreline. By mapping these ripples from the International Space Station, we’ve discovered that Earth’s weather doesn’t just end at the clouds, instead it reaches out beyond our planet, shaping the space weather that impacts our orbital economy.”

This artist’s conception depicts the Atmospheric Waves Experiment (AWE) scanning the atmosphere from aboard the International Space Station, measuring variations in infrared airglow to track atmospheric gravity waves as they move up from the lower atmosphere into space. Utah State University Space Dynamics Laboratory

During AWE’s 30-month residency on the station, the instrument captured four infrared images every second, tallying more than 80 million nighttime images, which is when airglow can be seen. It observed atmospheric gravity waves from numerous extreme weather events, including a tornado outbreak across the central U.S. in May 2024 and Hurricane Helene impacting the gulf coast of Florida in September 2024.

“We’ve seen atmospheric wave signatures associated with major terrestrial events, which provided a clear example of how intense weather systems can generate measurable upper-atmospheric responses,” said AWE’s principal investigator, Ludger Scherliess of Utah State University in Logan.

These events revealed variations in the types of atmospheric gravity waves created by different kinds of storms. For example, when AWE viewed atmospheric gravity waves generated by a thunderstorm in north Texas on May 26, 2024, it saw they were smaller and more irregular, with a notable asymmetry from north to south, compared to waves created by storms in the same part of the country earlier that month.

This image from AWE shows concentric atmospheric gravity waves caused by a severe weather event that included a tornado near the U.S.-Mexico border on May 3, 2024. Captured during orbit 2529 of AWE’s stay on the International Space Station, the image shows waves spreading across Texas and Mexico in near-perfect circles, a sight rarely observed with such clarity prior to the AWE mission. NASA/Utah State University

It is important to understand variations in the density of plasma, which is electrically charged gas, in Earth’s upper atmosphere instigated by atmospheric gravity waves, because these variations can disrupt radio signals traveling between satellites and the ground, and from satellite to satellite, degrading the accuracy and reliability of systems used for navigation, timing, and communications.

In a recent study, AWE measurements also revealed the gravity waves with the greatest influence on the upper atmosphere have small horizontal wavelengths, ranging from 30 to 300 kilometers, which AWE was specifically designed to measure.

With its data-collection phase complete, the AWE instrument was turned off to make way for another science experiment that will take its place on the outside of the space station. Called CLARREO Pathfinder (Calibration Absolute Radiance and Refractivity Observatory Pathfinder), the new instrument will take measurements of sunlight reflected by Earth and the Moon that are five to 10 times more accurate than those from existing sensors. The exchange of instruments is a key part of the space station’s mission and versatility as an orbiting laboratory for various types of research.

As the International Space Station traveled over the southeastern United States on Sept. 26, 2024, AWE observed atmospheric gravity waves generated by Hurricane Helene as the storm slammed into the gulf coast of Florida. The curved bands extending to the northwest of Florida, artificially colored red, yellow, and blue, show changes in brightness (or radiance) in a wavelength of infrared light produced by airglow in Earth’s mesosphere. The small black circles on the continent mark the locations of cities. Utah State University

In the coming days, a robotic arm on the space station, called Canadarm2, will remove the AWE instrument from its location. Soon afterward, the AWE instrument will be loaded into part of a SpaceX Dragon cargo spacecraft that will deorbit and burn up as it re-enters the atmosphere. However, all of AWE’s observations will ultimately become available to the public and the scientific community for ongoing research and discovery.

“Data from AWE will continue to be made public for both professional researchers and citizen scientists,” Scherliess said.

Some of this data already is available, including interactive, online visualizations on Utah State University’s website, where AWE’s observations are “painted” in swaths onto a globe or on a map as the space station orbits the planet. Users can rotate the visualizations to view atmospheric gravity waves from different angles.

A still image from an interactive visualization shows AWE data collected over the Western Hemisphere. Utah State University

Launched on Nov. 9, 2023, AWE is managed by the Explorers Program Office at NASA’s Goddard Space Flight Center in Greenbelt, Maryland. Utah State University’s Space Dynamics Laboratory built the AWE instrument and provided the mission operations center.

Hear more about AWE by listening to episode 334 of NASA’s Houston We Have a Podcast, recorded on Jan. 26, 2024.

By Vanessa Thomas

NASA’s Goddard Space Flight Center, Greenbelt, Md.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on NASA's successful completion of the AWE mission, which provided significant new scientific insights into how Earth's weather affects space weather. This has broad implications for protecting satellites and communication systems, benefiting society at large. The mission's data is publicly available for ongoing research, adding substantial value and impact.
articles.personality_title: NASA’s AWE mission reveals how Earth’s weather shapes space weather
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA’s Atmospheric Waves Experiment (AWE) was a special instrument installed on the International Space Station (ISS) in November 2023. Its job was to study atmospheric gravity waves, which are giant ripples in the atmosphere caused by strong winds or storms. These waves can travel upward from Earth’s weather into space, affecting satellites and communication signals.

**What happened** – On May 21, 2025, NASA turned off the AWE instrument after more than 30 months of collecting data—longer than its planned two-year mission. AWE took over 80 million nighttime images, capturing how storms like tornadoes and hurricanes created atmospheric gravity waves. It showed how weather events on Earth send ripples all the way to the edge of space.

**Impact** – AWE’s findings are important because they help scientists understand how Earth’s weather affects space weather. Space weather can disrupt satellites, GPS, and communication systems that people rely on every day. By mapping these waves, AWE showed that weather doesn’t just stay near the ground—it influences conditions far above us, which is new knowledge for protecting technology in space.

**What’s next step** – The AWE instrument will be removed from the ISS by a robotic arm and sent back to Earth’s atmosphere to burn up. It will be replaced by a new instrument called CLARREO Pathfinder, which will measure sunlight reflected by Earth and the Moon with much greater accuracy. Meanwhile, all of AWE’s data will be shared publicly so scientists and the public can continue learning from it.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s AWE mission successfully captured how Earth’s weather creates waves that reach into space, helping protect satellites and improve our understanding of space weather.
articles.personality_title_fr: La mission AWE de la NASA révèle comment la météo terrestre influence la météo spatiale
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’Atmospheric Waves Experiment (AWE) de la NASA était un instrument installé sur la Station spatiale internationale (ISS) en novembre 2023. Sa mission était d’étudier les ondes gravitationnelles atmosphériques, de grandes ondulations dans l’atmosphère causées par des vents forts ou des tempêtes. Ces ondes peuvent monter de la météo terrestre vers l’espace, affectant les satellites et les signaux de communication.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 21 mai 2025, la NASA a éteint l’instrument AWE après plus de 30 mois de collecte de données, dépassant sa mission initiale de deux ans. AWE a pris plus de 80 millions d’images nocturnes, montrant comment des tempêtes comme des tornades et des ouragans créent des ondes gravitationnelles atmosphériques. Il a démontré que les événements météorologiques envoient des ondulations jusqu’aux limites de l’espace.

**Impact** – Les découvertes d’AWE sont importantes car elles aident les scientifiques à comprendre comment la météo terrestre influence la météo spatiale. La météo spatiale peut perturber les satellites, le GPS et les systèmes de communication utilisés quotidiennement. En cartographiant ces ondes, AWE a montré que la météo ne reste pas près du sol, mais affecte aussi les conditions bien au-dessus de nous, une nouvelle connaissance pour protéger la technologie spatiale.

**Prochaines étapes** – L’instrument AWE sera retiré de l’ISS par un bras robotique et brûlera en rentrant dans l’atmosphère terrestre. Il sera remplacé par un nouvel instrument nommé CLARREO Pathfinder, qui mesurera la lumière solaire réfléchie par la Terre et la Lune avec une précision beaucoup plus grande. Par ailleurs, toutes les données d’AWE seront mises à disposition du public pour continuer les recherches.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La mission AWE de la NASA a capturé avec succès comment la météo terrestre crée des ondes qui atteignent l’espace, aidant à protéger les satellites et à mieux comprendre la météo spatiale.
articles.personality_title_es: La misión AWE de la NASA revela cómo el clima terrestre afecta el clima espacial
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El Experimento de Ondas Atmosféricas (AWE) de la NASA fue un instrumento instalado en la Estación Espacial Internacional (EEI) en noviembre de 2023. Su función era estudiar las ondas gravitacionales atmosféricas, grandes ondas en la atmósfera causadas por vientos fuertes o tormentas. Estas ondas pueden viajar desde el clima de la Tierra hacia el espacio, afectando satélites y señales de comunicación.

**Qué pasó** – El 21 de mayo de 2025, la NASA apagó el instrumento AWE después de más de 30 meses de recopilación de datos, superando su misión planificada de dos años. AWE tomó más de 80 millones de imágenes nocturnas, mostrando cómo tormentas como tornados y huracanes crean ondas gravitacionales atmosféricas. Demostró cómo los eventos climáticos envían ondas hasta el límite del espacio.

**Impacto** – Los hallazgos de AWE son importantes porque ayudan a los científicos a entender cómo el clima de la Tierra afecta al clima espacial. El clima espacial puede interrumpir satélites, GPS y sistemas de comunicación que usamos diariamente. Al mapear estas ondas, AWE mostró que el clima no se queda cerca del suelo, sino que influye en condiciones mucho más arriba, lo que es un nuevo conocimiento para proteger la tecnología espacial.

**Próximo paso** – El instrumento AWE será retirado de la EEI por un brazo robótico y se quemará al reingresar a la atmósfera terrestre. Será reemplazado por un nuevo instrumento llamado CLARREO Pathfinder, que medirá la luz solar reflejada por la Tierra y la Luna con mucha mayor precisión. Mientras tanto, todos los datos de AWE se compartirán públicamente para que científicos y el público sigan aprendiendo.

**Resumen en una frase** – La misión AWE de la NASA capturó con éxito cómo el clima terrestre crea ondas que llegan al espacio, ayudando a proteger satélites y mejorar la comprensión del clima espacial.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of the International Space Station orbiting Earth at night, gently scanning colorful, concentric ripples of glowing airglow waves rising from vivid storm patterns—tornadoes and hurricanes—visible beneath swirling clouds, with soft bands of infrared light spreading upward into space like ocean waves reaching a shoreline.

articles.title: Scientists discover towering red auroras reaching deep into space above Japan
articles.summary: Mysterious red auroras spotted over Japan were found reaching astonishingly high altitudes, even during space storms considered relatively mild. The discovery suggests hidden solar activity may be stronger than scientists realized — with potential consequences for satellites orbiting Earth.
articles.content:
On certain nights in Japan, people may notice a faint red glow spreading low across the horizon. The dim crimson haze can be easy to overlook, but it is created by powerful activity taking place far above Earth. Streams of charged particles from the Sun travel toward our planet and interact with Earth's magnetic field. When these particles collide with oxygen atoms high in the atmosphere, the atoms release energy as soft red light, producing rare auroras visible from the ground.

A new study published in the Journal of Space Weather and Space Climate reports that researchers from Hokkaido University and the Okinawa Institute of Science and Technology observed unusual red auroras above Japan at far greater heights than expected. The glowing displays extended roughly 500 to 800 kilometers above Earth's surface.

Rare Red Auroras Reach Extreme Heights

Auroras are most commonly seen near the polar regions, where charged solar particles interact with Earth's atmosphere to create shimmering lights in the sky. In Japan and other areas farther south, auroras are much less common and are usually linked to powerful geomagnetic storms. These events typically occur at altitudes of around 200 to 400 kilometers.

The new findings challenge that understanding.

"We found that red auroras can extend to extremely high altitudes even during those storms that are measured as moderately intense. I was really surprised because I didn't expect such tall auroras to appear even during moderately intense storms," says Tomohiro M. Nakayama, lead author of the study. "This suggests that these storms may actually be stronger than conventional indices indicate."

The research team studied five auroral events recorded in Hokkaido between June 2024 and March 2025. During those periods, bursts of charged particles from the Sun compressed Earth's magnetosphere, the invisible magnetic shield surrounding the planet. Although standard measurements classified the storms as moderate, the compression itself appeared unusually intense.

Solar Wind May Be Hiding Stronger Storms

Researchers believe dense streams of solar wind squeezed Earth's magnetic field so strongly that the upper atmosphere became heated and expanded upward. This may have pushed the region where red auroras form to much higher altitudes than scientists normally expect.

At the same time, the movement of charged particles may have masked the true intensity of the storms, making them appear weaker according to traditional space weather measurements.

To investigate the phenomenon, scientists combined satellite observations with photographs captured by citizen scientists across Japan. By studying the angles of the auroras in those images and mapping them along Earth's magnetic field lines, the researchers were able to estimate how high the glowing structures reached into the atmosphere.

The participation of skywatchers across the country proved especially valuable. Observations from multiple locations allowed the team to study rare auroral events in greater detail than would have been possible using conventional monitoring networks alone.

Why These Auroras Matter for Satellites

The findings may have important implications beyond the visual spectacle of auroras. When Earth's upper atmosphere heats up and expands, satellites orbiting the planet experience greater atmospheric drag. This extra resistance can gradually alter satellite trajectories and cause some spacecraft to lose altitude faster than expected.

"As the number of satellites in low Earth orbit continues to grow, understanding these effects is increasingly important," says Nakayama. "Our results could help improve space weather forecasting and support safer satellite operations."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about red auroras reaching much higher altitudes than previously known, revealing hidden solar activity that can impact satellite safety. This finding has broad implications for space weather forecasting and satellite operations, benefiting the growing number of satellites and thus society at large. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a positive advancement in understanding space weather phenomena.
articles.personality_title: Scientists discover towering red auroras reaching deep into space above Japan
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Auroras are colorful lights in the sky caused by charged particles from the Sun hitting Earth's atmosphere. They usually appear near the North and South Poles at heights between 200 and 400 kilometers. In Japan, auroras are rare and typically linked to strong space storms.

**What happened** – Researchers from Hokkaido University and the Okinawa Institute of Science and Technology studied red auroras seen over Japan between June 2024 and March 2025. They found these auroras reached heights of 500 to 800 kilometers, much higher than expected for moderate space storms. The team combined satellite data with photos taken by people across Japan to measure the auroras’ altitude.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that some space storms may be stronger than current measurements suggest. The intense solar wind pushed Earth's magnetic field and atmosphere higher, creating tall red auroras. This matters because when the atmosphere expands, it increases drag on satellites, which can change their orbits and affect their operation.

**What's next step** – Scientists will use these findings to improve space weather forecasts and satellite safety. By better understanding how solar storms affect the atmosphere, they can help protect satellites as more are launched into low Earth orbit.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New research reveals red auroras over Japan can reach much higher altitudes during moderate space storms, highlighting hidden solar activity that affects satellite safety.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent des aurores rouges géantes s'étendant loin dans l'espace au-dessus du Japon
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les aurores sont des lumières colorées dans le ciel causées par des particules chargées du Soleil qui frappent l'atmosphère terrestre. Elles apparaissent généralement près des pôles à des altitudes entre 200 et 400 kilomètres. Au Japon, les aurores sont rares et liées à de fortes tempêtes spatiales.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de l'Université de Hokkaido et de l'Institut des sciences et technologies d'Okinawa ont étudié des aurores rouges observées au-dessus du Japon entre juin 2024 et mars 2025. Ils ont découvert que ces aurores atteignaient des altitudes de 500 à 800 kilomètres, bien plus haut que prévu pour des tempêtes spatiales modérées. L'équipe a combiné des données satellites avec des photos prises par des citoyens au Japon pour mesurer la hauteur des aurores.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que certaines tempêtes spatiales sont plus fortes que ce que les mesures actuelles indiquent. Le vent solaire intense a poussé le champ magnétique et l'atmosphère terrestre vers le haut, créant de hautes aurores rouges. Cela est important car une atmosphère élargie augmente la résistance pour les satellites, ce qui peut modifier leur trajectoire et leur fonctionnement.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques utiliseront ces résultats pour améliorer les prévisions météorologiques spatiales et la sécurité des satellites. En comprenant mieux l'effet des tempêtes solaires sur l'atmosphère, ils pourront mieux protéger les satellites alors que leur nombre augmente en orbite basse.

**Phrase clé** – Une nouvelle recherche révèle que les aurores rouges au-dessus du Japon peuvent atteindre des altitudes beaucoup plus grandes pendant des tempêtes spatiales modérées, montrant une activité solaire cachée qui impacte la sécurité des satellites.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren auroras rojas gigantes que llegan profundo al espacio sobre Japón
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las auroras son luces de colores en el cielo causadas por partículas cargadas del Sol que chocan con la atmósfera de la Tierra. Normalmente se ven cerca de los polos a alturas entre 200 y 400 kilómetros. En Japón, las auroras son raras y suelen estar relacionadas con tormentas espaciales fuertes.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Hokkaido y del Instituto de Ciencia y Tecnología de Okinawa estudiaron auroras rojas vistas sobre Japón entre junio de 2024 y marzo de 2025. Descubrieron que estas auroras alcanzaron alturas de 500 a 800 kilómetros, mucho más alto de lo esperado para tormentas espaciales moderadas. El equipo combinó datos de satélites con fotos tomadas por ciudadanos en Japón para medir la altura de las auroras.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo muestra que algunas tormentas espaciales son más fuertes de lo que indican las mediciones actuales. El viento solar intenso empujó el campo magnético y la atmósfera de la Tierra hacia arriba, creando auroras rojas muy altas. Esto es importante porque una atmósfera más grande aumenta la resistencia para los satélites, lo que puede cambiar sus órbitas y afectar su funcionamiento.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos usarán estos resultados para mejorar las predicciones del clima espacial y la seguridad de los satélites. Al entender mejor cómo las tormentas solares afectan la atmósfera, podrán proteger mejor los satélites a medida que se lanzan más al espacio cercano a la Tierra.

**Una frase para recordar** – Una nueva investigación muestra que las auroras rojas sobre Japón pueden alcanzar alturas mucho mayores durante tormentas espaciales moderadas, revelando actividad solar oculta que afecta la seguridad de los satélites.
articles.image_prompt: A serene night landscape over Japan with softly glowing, towering red aurora curtains stretching upward into a deep starry sky, their luminous tendrils weaving through abstract magnetic field lines symbolized by gentle, flowing arcs of light, while distant silhouettes of mountains and a calm horizon frame the scene in warm, natural tones.
